policy_statement: Develop City of Austin design/construction specifications and purchasing agreements to deliver healthy, low-carbon buildings and prioritize healthy building strategies in community facilities serving low-income communities and communities of color first.
mechanism_description: Adopting government building design/construction standards and procurement requirements drives lower-energy, lower-carbon public facilities, reducing building-related greenhouse gas emissions.
secondary_justification: Resource Efficiency: Higher-performance building standards/procurement reduce energy input needed to deliver the same indoor comfort and building services (lighting/heating/cooling/ventilation), i.e., same service → less energy consumed.
primary_causal_pathway: Building design/procurement requirements → ↓ building energy demand (electricity + fuels) → ↓ CO2e from power generation and on-site combustion
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: The city changes how public buildings are specified, designed, constructed, and operated by adopting mandatory standards and procurement criteria (e.g., efficiency thresholds, performance specs, commissioning requirements).  
(2) Process: Improved building envelope, mechanical systems, and controls reduce heating/cooling/lighting loads and wasted energy during operation.  
(3) Outcome: Lower operational energy consumption reduces combustion of fossil fuels on-site and at power plants, cutting operational GHG emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced building operational energy use, leading to lower direct and indirect greenhouse gas emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway is operational energy reduction in buildings leading to lower GHG emissions; this is classic demand-side mitigation.  
2) Mitigation is primary over Resource Efficiency because the principal climate objective/outcome is emissions reduction (CO2e), while efficiency is the means.  
3) Secondary category Resource Efficiency is inherent because reducing operational emissions in this mechanism necessarily occurs through reduced energy inputs per unit of building service (or cleaner energy use coupled with reduced demand), making efficiency an unavoidable co-effect of the standard/procurement approach.  
4) Edge cases: Some “green building” programs also emphasize low-embodied-carbon materials, which would shift/expand the mechanism toward embodied emissions; however, this canonical mechanism is explicitly about operational emissions reduction.  
5) Labels apply broadly: any jurisdiction using standards/procurement to improve operational building performance will reduce energy use and thus operational emissions irrespective of location (grid mix affects magnitude, not direction).
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a policy branded as “green building standards” primarily targets embodied carbon/material procurement rather than operational energy (or primarily targets resilience/health outcomes without energy implications), it may require a different primary mechanism/category; here the specified mechanism is operational emissions reduction.
key_indicators: “design and construction specifications”, “purchasing agreements”, “healthy, low-carbon buildings”, “Lead by example through design and construction standards”, “prioritize in community centers, libraries, and other community facilities”
co_benefits: Health/indoor air quality improvements and equity benefits (“healthy” buildings; prioritization for “low-income communities and communities of color”), potential resilience improvements (e.g., better thermal comfort during heat) but not stated as hazard-specific adaptation.
instance_edge_case_notes: Instrument could also be interpreted as a regulatory-mandate (if specifications are mandatory internal standards), but the explicit mention of “purchasing agreements” makes procurement-requirement the best fit; “healthy” suggests IAQ/health co-benefits rather than a separate adaptation category without hazard context.
